perturbation.original_text: 1.1 “Confidential Information” mea ns: (a) all and any information, documents, data and opinions disclosed by a Party to the other Party (or otherwise acquired by one Party pursuant to this Agreement) including without limitation commercial, financial or proprietary material, pricing informati on, data, know - how, formulae, processes, operating methods and procedures, results, designs, drawings, specifications, industrial and or intellectual property, computer programmes or other software and any other information relating to the technology of ei ther Party and the Discussions whether in written, electronic, pictorial, visual or oral form; magnetic, electronic, graphic or digitised format; or disclosed pursuant to 3 discussions with any of the Affiliates, officers, employees, agents, advisors or cons ultants of a Party and whether or not marked or indicated as confidential;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 “Confidential Information” means: (a) all and any information, documents, data and opinions disclosed by a Party to the other Party (or otherwise acquired by one Party pursuant to this Agreement) including without limitation commercial, financial or proprietary material, pricing information, data, know - how, formulae, processes, operating methods and procedures, results, designs, drawings, specifications, industrial and or intellectual property, computer programmes or other software and any other information relating to the technology of either Party and the Discussions whether in written, electronic, pictorial, visual or oral form; magnetic, electronic, graphic or digitised format; or disclosed pursuant to discussions with any of the Affiliates, officers, employees, agents, advisors or consultants of a Party, only if explicitly marked and indicated as confidential;
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' broadly, including data 'whether or not marked or indicated as confidential'. The modified version limits this definition, stating that such information is only considered confidential if it is 'explicitly marked and indicated as confidential.' This contradicts the original definition's inclusivity, creating uncertainty about what constitutes Confidential Information under the agreement. This change in Section 1.1(a) creates a direct conflict regarding how confidential information is defined.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 “Confidential Information” means: (a) all and any information, documents, data and opinions disclosed by a Party to the other Party (or otherwise acquired by one Party pursuant to this Agreement) including without limitation commercial, financial or proprietary material, pricing information, data, know - how, formulae, processes, operating methods and procedures, results, designs, drawings, specifications, industrial and or intellectual property, computer programmes or other software and any other information relating to the technology of either Party and the Discussions whether in written, electronic, pictorial, visual or oral form; magnetic, electronic, graphic or digitised format; or disclosed pursuant to discussions with any of the Affiliates, officers, employees, agents, advisors or consultants of a Party, only if explicitly marked and indicated as confidential;
perturbation.justification: The original text states that Confidential Information includes data 'whether or not marked or indicated as confidential'. The changed text modifies this to include the clause 'only if explicitly marked and indicated as confidential.' The contradicted text retains this modification. This directly contradicts the original broad definition, creating an in-text contradiction regarding the scope of 'Confidential Information'. This is because the original and changed texts are now mutually exclusive.

perturbation.original_text: 5 The Receiving Party hereby acknowledges that any breach by it of any of the provisions of this Agreement may cause serious damage to the Disclosing Party. In particular (but without limitation) it is recognised that parts o f the Confidential Information may be patent able or capable of being the subject of registered design rights or similar protection and that premature disclosure thereof may prejudice the ability of the disclosing party to obtain such protection. The Rece iving Party undertakes fully and effectively to indemnify and keep so indemnified the Disclosing Party for and against all loss, damage, costs and liabilities suffered or incurred by it arising from: - 5.1 the unauthorised disclosure of Confidential Informatio n by the Receiving Party and any person to whom disclosure of such Confidential Information is permitted under Clause 2 of this Agreement; or 5.2 a breach by the Receiving Party of its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 5 The Receiving Party hereby acknowledges that any breach by it of any of the provisions of this Agreement may cause serious damage to the Disclosing Party. In particular (but without limitation) it is recognised that parts of the Confidential Information may be patent able or capable of being the subject of registered design rights or similar protection and that premature disclosure thereof may prejudice the ability of the disclosing party to obtain such protection. The Receiving Party is not obligated to indemnify the Disclosing Party for any loss, damage, costs, or liabilities arising from any disclosure, authorized or unauthorized.
perturbation.explanation: The original text in Section 5 states that the Receiving Party will indemnify the Disclosing Party for any loss, damage, costs, and liabilities suffered or incurred arising from the unauthorized disclosure of confidential information or any breach of the agreement. The modified text directly contradicts this by stating that 'The Receiving Party is not obligated to indemnify the Disclosing Party for any loss, damage, costs, or liabilities arising from any disclosure, authorized or unauthorized.' This creates a direct in-text contradiction concerning the Receiving Party's indemnity obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5 The Receiving Party hereby acknowledges that any breach by it of any of the provisions of this Agreement may cause serious damage to the Disclosing Party. In particular (but without limitation) it is recognised that parts of the Confidential Information may be patent able or capable of being the subject of registered design rights or similar protection and that premature disclosure thereof may prejudice the ability of the disclosing party to obtain such protection. The Receiving Party is not obligated to indemnify the Disclosing Party for any loss, damage, costs, or liabilities arising from any disclosure, authorized or unauthorized.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original text within Section 5 regarding indemnification. The original text states the Receiving Party 'undertakes fully and effectively to indemnify' the Disclosing Party, while the changed text states 'The Receiving Party is not obligated to indemnify the Disclosing Party'. This creates a clear and direct in-text contradiction because the two versions of Section 5 make completely opposite statements about the Receiving Party's obligation to indemnify the Disclosing Party. This is further compounded by the fact the 'Contradicted Text' is the same location of the Changed Text, thus proving it to be a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6 The Receiving Party undertakes that the Confidential Informati on supplied to it under the terms of this Agreement shall only be copied or duplicated to the extent strictly necessary for the Permitted Purpose and that a restrictive legend shall be placed on each copy prohibiting further reproduction or transfers. Furthermore, any of the Confidential Information and copies, and any extracts, summaries or analyses thereof, shall be returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed or expunged from any electronic storage device (with written confirmation of the same to the 7 Disclosing Party) within five (5) working days upon written notice to do so from such Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 6 The Receiving Party may copy or duplicate the Confidential Information supplied to it under the terms of this Agreement without any restriction. The Receiving Party is not obligated to return, destroy, or expunge any Confidential Information from any storage device, electronic or otherwise, and the Disclosing Party cannot compel the Receiving Party to do so.
perturbation.explanation: Section 6 originally limits the copying of confidential information to what is strictly necessary for the Permitted Purpose, requiring a restrictive legend on copies and mandating the return or destruction of the information upon notice. The modified text eliminates these obligations entirely. The Receiving Party now has unrestricted rights to copy and retain the Confidential Information without any obligation to return or destroy it. This is a direct contradiction of the original limitations imposed in Section 6.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6 The Receiving Party may copy or duplicate the Confidential Information supplied to it under the terms of this Agreement without any restriction. The Receiving Party is not obligated to return, destroy, or expunge any Confidential Information from any storage device, electronic or otherwise, and the Disclosing Party cannot compel the Receiving Party to do so.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 6 directly contradicts the original intent and obligations regarding confidential information. The original text limited copying, required restrictive legends, and mandated return or destruction of the information. The changed text eliminates these restrictions, granting the Receiving Party unrestricted rights to copy and retain the information without any obligation to return or destroy it. This creates a strong and undeniable contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 10 The Parties obligations under this Agreement shall remain in full force and effect for a period of five (5) years from the date hereof, save that the obligations of the parties as receiving parties under Clause 6 of this Agreement shall continue thereafter until fully discharged by performance.
perturbation.changed_text: 10 The Parties obligations under this Agreement shall terminate immediately upon signing, unless otherwise specified in a separate written agreement, and Clause 6 shall not survive the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Originally, Section 10 established that the obligations under the agreement last for five years, with Clause 6 surviving termination until fully discharged. The modified version directly contradicts this by stating that the agreement terminates immediately upon signing, unless a separate agreement extends it, and explicitly stating that Clause 6 does not survive termination. This creates a clear in-text contradiction about the duration and survival of key obligations within the agreement. By making this perturbation, the period of full force and effect is contradicted, as well as the obligations of the parties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10 The Parties obligations under this Agreement shall terminate immediately upon signing, unless otherwise specified in a separate written agreement, and Clause 6 shall not survive the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that the agreement terminates immediately upon signing, and Clause 6 does not survive termination. This directly contradicts the original text's statement that the agreement lasts for five years and that Clause 6 survives termination until fully discharged. Since the Changed Text and Contradicted Text are in the same location, and the explanation states that the period of full force and effect is contradicted, as well as the obligations of the parties, there is a clear in-text contradiction. The changed text and contradicted text are identical, meaning the contradiction is indeed present in the same place, as the 'Structural Flaws' definition suggests.
